skill.name: alembic
skill.description: Guidance for creating, applying, reviewing, and troubleshooting Alembic migrations in SQLAlchemy projects.
skill.summary: Manage SQLAlchemy database schema changes with Alembic migration workflows.
skill.author: bossjones

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged Markdown code fences, Alembic command examples, and database URL configuration snippets in SKILL.md. Review found these are documentation examples for expected migration workflows, not hidden execution, exfiltration, weak cryptography, or prompt injection. The main residual risk is operational: users could run migration commands that alter or downgrade a database.

low_findings.title: Operational Database Migration Commands
low_findings.description: The skill provides Alembic command examples for revision creation, upgrades, downgrades, branch merges, initialization, and dependency installation. These are legitimate for an Alembic guide, but running them against the wrong environment can change or remove database schema state.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are visible, relevant to the stated migration-management purpose, and not hidden. Confidence is high that this is legitimate documentation with low operational risk rather than malicious execution.

low_findings.title: Database URL Configuration Example
low_findings.description: The skill shows how to read a project database URL and pass it to Alembic or SQLAlchemy. This is expected migration configuration and no evidence shows the URL being logged, transmitted to a third party, or otherwise exfiltrated.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The database URL appears only in local configuration and engine creation examples. No network destination, logging sink, or secret collection behavior is present in the reviewed file.

dangerous_patterns.title: False Positive: Markdown Backtick Code Fences
dangerous_patterns.description: The static analyzer classified Markdown fenced examples as Ruby or shell backtick execution. The reviewed lines are documentation blocks containing bash or Python examples, not executable skill code.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Line-number review confirms these are Markdown fences and examples in SKILL.md. There is no Ruby source file or runtime backtick execution path.

dangerous_patterns.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: Static analysis reported weak cryptography at the skill metadata and introductory Alembic description. No cryptographic API, digest algorithm, hashing function, or encryption logic appears at those locations.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain descriptive prose about Alembic and database migrations. They do not contain MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or any cryptographic operation.

dangerous_patterns.title: False Positive: Environment Secret Access
dangerous_patterns.description: The database URL examples configure Alembic and SQLAlchemy connections for migrations. The reviewed file does not instruct the assistant to collect secrets, print credentials, or send environment data elsewhere.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The usage is semantically tied to local migration setup. The file contains no exfiltration endpoint, credential harvesting instruction, or prompt injection around these snippets.

content.value_statement: Database schema changes need repeatable review and safe rollout steps. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code guide Alembic migration workflows for SQLAlchemy projects.
actual_capabilities: Explains how to create Alembic revisions with autogenerate and manual messages.
Shows upgrade, downgrade, current, history, and revision inspection commands.
Provides async SQLAlchemy env.py configuration patterns for Alembic.
Documents manual operations for columns, tables, indexes, constraints, and batch mode.
Covers branch migrations, merge revisions, and up-to-date database checks.
Includes examples for running Alembic commands programmatically from Python.
limitations: Does not inspect a live database or verify migration output by itself.
Does not replace project-specific migration review or backup procedures.
Examples need adaptation for each application model layout and database driver.
Downgrade and data migration commands can be destructive if users run them carelessly.

use_cases.title: Create Safe Schema Revisions
use_cases.description: Generate and review Alembic migration plans before applying model changes to a database.

use_cases.title: Maintain Migration Configuration
use_cases.description: Set up env.py for async SQLAlchemy, target metadata, filtering, batch mode, and custom generation rules.

use_cases.title: Troubleshoot Migration State
use_cases.description: Check current revisions, compare heads, inspect history, and handle branch or merge migration workflows.

prompt_templates.title: Create a First Migration
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a SQLAlchemy project
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me create my first Alembic migration for a SQLAlchemy project. Explain the commands and what I should review before upgrading.

prompt_templates.title: Review an Autogenerated Revision
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my Alembic autogenerate plan for risky schema changes. Focus on nullable columns, indexes, constraints, and downgrade behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Configure Async Alembic
prompt_templates.prompt: Guide me through configuring Alembic env.py for async SQLAlchemy with target metadata, database URL handling, and migration execution.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Branch Merge
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me resolve multiple Alembic migration heads. Explain how to inspect branches, create a merge revision, and verify the final state.

output_examples.input: I need to add an email column and index to users.
output_examples.output: A migration review checklist covering the nullable column, index naming, backfill needs, upgrade order, and downgrade reversal.

output_examples.input: My SQLite migration cannot alter a table normally.
output_examples.output: Guidance to use Alembic batch mode, with notes about table recreation behavior and testing on a copy of the database.

output_examples.input: Two feature branches created separate Alembic heads.
output_examples.output: A branch-resolution plan that checks current heads, reviews both histories, creates a merge revision, and verifies a single head.

best_practices: Review autogenerated migrations before running them against shared or production databases.
Back up important data and test upgrades and downgrades in a staging environment first.
Keep migration files small, reversible when possible, and aligned with application model changes.
anti_patterns: Do not run downgrade commands on production without a tested recovery plan.
Do not commit autogenerated migrations without reading every operation they contain.
Do not hardcode production database credentials in alembic.ini or migration files.

faq.question: Does this skill run database migrations automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples. Users decide which commands to run in their own environment.

faq.question: Can it help with async SQLAlchemy projects?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill includes an async env.py pattern using SQLAlchemy async engine configuration.

faq.question: Does it support SQLite migrations?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains batch mode, which is commonly needed for SQLite schema changes.

faq.question: Can it review autogenerated migrations?
faq.answer: Yes. It can guide review of generated operations, empty migrations, indexes, constraints, and downgrade steps.

faq.question: Does it store or transmit database credentials?
faq.answer: No evidence was found that the skill stores, logs, or transmits credentials. It only shows local configuration examples.

faq.question: What should I do before applying migrations?
faq.answer: Review the migration file, test it on staging data, confirm backups, and verify the target database environment.
